walkthrough.title: Choose Places of Power
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Res Arcana. First, take the five double-sided Place of Power tiles. Each tile has a different Place of Power on each side. For your first game, pick one side of each tile at random and lay all five in the center of the table. These are powerful locations that players will compete to claim during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Monument Display
walkthrough.text: Now find the ten Monument cards. Shuffle them and reveal a number based on your player count. For two players, use a smaller display. For three or four players, lay out the full display. Place the revealed monuments face up near the Places of Power. Monuments cost four gold each and are worth victory points.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Magic Items
walkthrough.text: Find all eight Magic Item cards and lay them face up in a row near the center of the table. These are special one-use-per-round abilities that players will select when they pass. Every Magic Item is available to everyone, but only one player can hold each item at a time.

walkthrough.title: Create the Essence Supply
walkthrough.text: Let's set out the essence tokens. There are five types: gold, elan which are the red tokens, calm which are the green tokens, death which are the black tokens, and life which are the blue tokens. Place all tokens in a shared supply in the middle of the table where everyone can reach them. These essences are the currency of the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Victory Point Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place all the victory point tokens in a central supply near the essence pool. Players will accumulate VP from Places of Power, monuments, and certain artifacts throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Deal Mage Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the ten Mage cards and deal two face down to each player. Look at both of your Mages, then choose one to keep and discard the other face down. Your Mage defines your starting ability and often your starting essences. Place your chosen Mage face up in front of you.

walkthrough.title: Deal and Draft Artifacts
walkthrough.text: Now deal eight artifact cards to each player. For your first game, you can skip the drafting and just keep all eight cards. For experienced players, use the full draft: choose one card and pass the rest to the left. Keep choosing one and passing until all eight are drafted. These eight artifacts are your entire personal deck for the game.

walkthrough.title: Select Starting Magic Item
walkthrough.text: Each player now selects one Magic Item from the display. Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each person takes one Magic Item. Place it face up in front of you. This is your Magic Item for the first round. You will swap it when you pass.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Check your Mage card for any starting essences listed on it. Take those essences from the supply. Some Mages start with two or three essences of specific types. Others start with none but have stronger abilities.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Take your eight drafted artifact cards. Draw three as your opening hand. The remaining five form your personal draw pile, placed face down in front of you. You will draw from this pile throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly determine who goes first. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. Each player should have their Mage in play, one Magic Item, three cards in hand, five cards in their draw pile, and their starting essences. You are ready to begin. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Res Arcana is a tight engine-building game where you race to ten victory points. The game plays over multiple rounds, each consisting of an essence collection phase followed by an action phase where players take turns. The first player to reach ten or more VP at the end of a round triggers the game end.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round has two phases. First, everyone simultaneously collects essences from their Mage, artifacts in play, and any Places of Power they own. Then players take turns clockwise, performing one action at a time. When you cannot or choose not to take any more actions, you pass and select a new Magic Item for next round.

walkthrough.title: Collecting Essences
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, look at your Mage card, your artifacts in play, and any Places of Power you have claimed. Each of these may show essence icons that you collect. Take the matching essences from the supply. This is your income for the round. As you build your engine by placing artifacts and claiming Places of Power, your income grows each round.

walkthrough.title: Action: Place an Artifact
walkthrough.text: To place an artifact from your hand into play, pay its essence cost shown in the upper left corner. Once placed, the artifact stays in play permanently. It will generate essences at the start of future rounds and may provide abilities you can activate. Building your artifact engine is the core of the game.

walkthrough.title: Action: Use a Power
walkthrough.text: Many artifacts, Mages, Places of Power, and Magic Items have tap abilities. To use one, tap it by turning it sideways. This shows it has been used this round. Tap abilities can generate essences, convert one type to another, score VP, or create other effects. Each tap ability can only be used once per round.

walkthrough.title: Action: Discard for Essences
walkthrough.text: If you are short on essences, you can discard an artifact card from your hand to gain one gold essence and one other essence of your choice. This is an emergency move because discarding reduces your future options. Your deck is only eight cards, so every discard hurts. Use this when you absolutely need essences to make a crucial play.

walkthrough.title: Action: Claim a Place of Power
walkthrough.text: Places of Power are the most valuable assets in the game. Each one has a specific essence cost to claim. When you claim one, place it in front of you. It now generates essences for you each round and is worth VP as printed. Most Places of Power also have powerful tap abilities. You can claim multiple Places of Power over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Action: Buy a Monument
walkthrough.text: Monuments cost four gold essences each and are worth a fixed amount of victory points, typically one or two VP. When you buy one, place it in front of you. Monuments are a flexible path to VP because gold is the most convertible essence. The monument display is limited, so desirable ones can sell out.

walkthrough.title: Action: Pass
walkthrough.text: When you pass, you are done acting for the round. Return your current Magic Item to the display and select a new one. The first player to pass gets first pick of Magic Items, which is a significant advantage. Sometimes passing early to grab the perfect Magic Item is better than squeezing out one more action.

walkthrough.title: Magic Items Explained
walkthrough.text: Magic Items are powerful one-use-per-round effects. Some convert essences, some grant extra resources, some let you attack opponents, and some let you draw cards. You swap your Magic Item every round when you pass. Choosing the right Magic Item for your strategy can make or break your engine.

walkthrough.title: Attacking and Defense
walkthrough.text: Some cards and abilities let you attack opponents, forcing them to discard essences. Dragons are the primary attack mechanism. When you are attacked, shields on your artifacts and Places of Power can absorb the damage. Each shield icon blocks one point of attack damage. If you see opponents building dragons, consider investing in shields.

walkthrough.title: The Essence Economy
walkthrough.text: The economy in Res Arcana is deliberately tight. Every essence matters, and you will rarely have a surplus. Gold is the most versatile essence because it buys monuments and many costs accept gold. Conversion abilities that turn one essence into another are extremely valuable. Plan your spending carefully because running out of essences means passing with nothing accomplished.

walkthrough.title: Victory Check
walkthrough.text: After all players have passed, check if any player has ten or more victory points. VP comes from Places of Power, monuments, artifact VP, and certain effects. If one or more players have reached ten VP, the game ends and the player with the highest VP total wins. If nobody has reached ten, untap all tapped cards, and begin a new round.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Tiebreakers
walkthrough.text: The game ends the moment a victory check reveals someone at ten or more VP. The player with the most VP wins. If players are tied, the player with the most total essences remaining breaks the tie. Games are typically fast, often ending in five to seven rounds. Someone will reach ten VP sooner than you expect.

10+ VP at end of round triggers game end

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Workshop
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three players. Each player has a different Mage and a different strategy forming. We will see smart plays and common mistakes. Let's get started.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Mages
walkthrough.text: Player one has the Alchemist, who is excellent at converting essences. Player two has the Necromancer, who specializes in death essences. Player three has the Druid, who works well with life and calm essences. Each player has drawn three artifact cards into their hand and collected their Mage's starting essences.

walkthrough.title: Collecting Essences
walkthrough.text: Let's begin the round. Everyone simultaneously collects essences from their Mage. Player one's Alchemist provides one gold. Player two's Necromancer provides one death essence. Player three's Druid provides one calm and one life essence. Since no one has artifacts in play yet, that is all the income for round one.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Place an Artifact
walkthrough.text: They pay the gold and place the artifact into play. This artifact generates one elan essence each round. Player one now has a permanent income source that will pay off every future round.

walkthrough.title: Why Engine Building Matters Early
walkthrough.text: Player one made the right call. In Res Arcana, every round your artifacts generate essences, so placing them early means more total income over the game. An artifact placed in round one generates essences for potentially six more rounds. The same artifact placed in round four only generates for two or three.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Place an Artifact
walkthrough.text: This artifact has a tap ability that converts one death essence into two gold. Player two now has a conversion engine forming around death essences, which synergizes perfectly with the Necromancer.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Hoarding Essences
walkthrough.text: Player three has two calm and two life essences but decides to hold onto them, hoping to save up for a Place of Power next round. They pass without placing any artifacts. Meanwhile, Players one and two have artifacts generating income for them.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Before You Buy
walkthrough.text: Hoarding essences while opponents build their engines is a classic Res Arcana mistake. Player three's saved essences will not grow on their own. If they had placed a cheap artifact this round, that artifact would generate essences every future round, actually getting them to their Place of Power faster through accumulated income.

walkthrough.title: Player One Uses a Power
walkthrough.text: Player one's Alchemist has a tap ability that converts one essence of any type into one essence of any other type. They tap the Alchemist to turn a leftover calm essence into a gold essence. Conversion abilities are incredibly powerful in this game because they let you transform what you have into what you need.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Taps Their Artifact
walkthrough.text: Player two taps the creature artifact they just placed, converting one death essence into two gold. They now have gold building up to buy a monument or save toward a Place of Power. Player two's death-to-gold pipeline is already producing.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Discarding Too Much
walkthrough.text: Player three is short on the essences they need to place their best artifact. Instead of waiting or playing a cheaper card, they discard two artifacts from their hand for emergency essences. That is four essences gained but two of their eight cards permanently gone. Their future options just shrank dramatically.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Protect Your Deck
walkthrough.text: Your deck is only eight cards. Every card you discard is a card that can never generate essences, score VP, or provide abilities. Discarding one card in a pinch is acceptable. Discarding two or more in the early game is usually a sign that your draft did not support your Mage or that you are trying to rush a strategy that is not ready yet.

walkthrough.title: Passing and Magic Item Selection
walkthrough.text: Player three passes first and gets first pick of Magic Items for next round. They grab the item that provides two life essences, which fits their Druid strategy. Player one passes next and takes a conversion item. Player two passes last and takes what remains.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Passing Order Matters
walkthrough.text: In Res Arcana, the first player to pass gets the best Magic Item choice. Sometimes passing early to secure the perfect item is worth more than one extra action. Player three accidentally benefited from their passive round by getting first pick, but it would have been even better to build an artifact AND then pass early.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Check
walkthrough.text: After everyone has passed, we check for ten VP. No one is close this early, so untap all cards and begin round two. Notice how Players one and two will now collect more essences than last round because they have artifacts in play. Player three's income is still just their Mage.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Ignoring Places of Power
walkthrough.text: By round three, Player three has been buying monuments with gold but has not claimed any Places of Power. Places of Power are worth more VP and often provide powerful abilities. Player two claimed a Place of Power in round two and is generating three extra essences per round from it. Player three's monument strategy is too slow.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Prioritize Places of Power
walkthrough.text: Places of Power are the most VP-efficient purchases in the game. They provide ongoing income, powerful abilities, and significant VP. Monuments are fine as supplementary VP, but relying solely on monuments is a losing strategy because they provide no ongoing engine benefit. Always evaluate which Places of Power your essences can reach.

walkthrough.title: How the Game Develops
walkthrough.text: Rounds progress quickly in Res Arcana. By round three or four, players have several artifacts in play generating robust income. Places of Power start getting claimed. The race to ten VP accelerates. Someone will cross the finish line by round five, six, or seven. The player with the best engine and the smartest claims usually wins.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how to build an engine with artifacts, convert essences with abilities, and compete for Places of Power. Remember the three big lessons: build artifacts early for compound income, protect your eight-card deck, and prioritize Places of Power over monuments.

walkthrough.title: Before You Cast
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on drafting and resource management.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to essence types and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Cast

Reference the Appendix → essence types and key terms

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest traps new players fall into. First, hoarding essences instead of placing artifacts early. Your income does not grow unless you build your engine. Second, discarding too many cards from your eight-card deck. Each discard permanently removes future options. Third, ignoring Places of Power in favor of monuments. Places of Power provide ongoing income and abilities, not just VP.

walkthrough.title: Draft for Your Mage
walkthrough.text: During the draft, your Mage should guide your picks. If your Mage produces death essences, draft artifacts that cost death essences or convert death into other types. If your Mage has a conversion ability, draft artifacts with varied costs that your Mage can help pay for. A tight synergy between Mage and artifacts is the foundation of every winning strategy.

walkthrough.title: Identify Your Path Early
walkthrough.text: Res Arcana rewards players who commit to a plan. In the first round, decide whether you are racing toward a Place of Power, accumulating gold for monuments, or building a VP engine through artifacts. Trying to do all three usually means you do none well. Look at the available Places of Power, check which ones match your essence production, and build toward the most accessible one.

walkthrough.title: Engine Before Victory Points
walkthrough.text: In the first two or three rounds, focus entirely on building income. Place artifacts, use conversion abilities, and grow your essence generation. Do not buy monuments or claim Places of Power until your engine can sustain it. A strong engine in round three can claim a Place of Power and buy a monument in a single round, while a weak engine is still struggling to place a second artifact.

walkthrough.title: Tempo Awareness
walkthrough.text: Someone will reach ten VP faster than you expect, often by round five or six. Keep track of what opponents are building. If Player two has seven VP and strong income, you cannot afford to spend two more rounds building your engine. Sometimes you need to pivot from engine mode to scoring mode even if your engine is not perfect. The game does not wait for you to get ready.

walkthrough.title: Magic Item Strategy
walkthrough.text: Your Magic Item should fill a gap in your economy. If you produce plenty of colored essences but lack gold, take a Magic Item that provides gold or converts to gold. If you need a burst of a specific essence to claim a Place of Power, choose accordingly. The Magic Item you select when passing should serve your next round's most important play.

walkthrough.title: Gold Is King
walkthrough.text: Gold is the most flexible essence in the game. It buys monuments, pays many artifact costs, and converts easily. If you are unsure what to collect, gold is rarely wrong. Artifacts and abilities that produce or convert into gold give you the flexibility to pivot between strategies. A gold-rich player can respond to whatever opportunities arise.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Pass
walkthrough.text: Passing is a strategic action, not a surrender. Passing first gives you first pick of Magic Items. If you have accomplished your main goal for the round and the remaining actions are marginal, pass early. If you are building toward a critical play and need one more action to complete it, staying in is worth the worse Magic Item selection.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't hoard essences — build engine first
Don't discard too many cards — 8-card deck is tiny
Don't skip Places of Power — they're the best VP source

Tight Mage-artifact synergy = foundation of every win

walkthrough.title: Draft Archetypes
walkthrough.text: There are several proven draft archetypes in Res Arcana. The death engine focuses on death-producing artifacts and Places of Power that convert death into VP. The gold rush prioritizes gold generation to buy monuments quickly. The creature swarm plays multiple cheap creatures for broad income. The dragon aggression uses attack cards to disrupt opponents while building your own VP. Identify which archetype your draft is shaping toward and commit.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table
walkthrough.text: During the draft, pay attention to which cards are being passed to you. If you see multiple death-themed artifacts, other players are probably avoiding death, which means the Necromancer and death Places of Power may be uncontested. Conversely, if you see few gold producers, gold will be scarce, and the monument path may be risky. Adapt your picks to the draft signals.

walkthrough.title: Place of Power Priority Rankings
walkthrough.text: Not all Places of Power are created equal. The ones that generate essences and score VP through tap abilities are usually the strongest because they provide both income and scoring in one asset. Places that only provide static VP are weaker because they do not grow your engine. Evaluate each Place of Power by asking: how much does it cost, how much income does it generate, and how quickly does it pay for itself?

walkthrough.title: The Speed-to-Ten Calculation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players constantly calculate their fastest path to ten VP. Count your current VP, then identify the cheapest way to add the remaining points. Is claiming a Place of Power cheaper than buying three monuments? Can you reach ten this round by using a tap ability? Keep this calculation running every round. The player who finds the shortest path usually wins.

walkthrough.title: Denying Opponents
walkthrough.text: In a tight game, claiming a Place of Power your opponent needs can be more valuable than taking the one that fits you best. If Player two has been building toward the Death Place of Power all game, claiming it out from under them even at an essence premium can strand their entire strategy. This is especially powerful in two-player games where denials hit harder.

walkthrough.title: Pass Timing Optimization
walkthrough.text: The decision of when to pass is one of the deepest in the game. Passing first gives you the best Magic Item, which can be worth two or three essences. Passing last means you took more actions, potentially placing an extra artifact or tapping an ability one more time. Calculate the value of the next action versus the value of the ideal Magic Item. In rounds where a specific item enables your next-round plan, passing early is often correct.

walkthrough.title: Artifact Cost Curve
walkthrough.text: Your eight-card deck should include a mix of costs. One or two free or one-cost artifacts let you place something in round one without stress. Two or three medium-cost artifacts form your mid-game engine. One or two expensive powerhouse artifacts cap off your engine in later rounds. If every card in your deck costs four or five essences, you will not be able to play anything until round three.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Conversion Chains
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds, efficient conversion chains can generate surprising VP bursts. A chain might look like: tap Mage to convert essences, tap artifact to generate gold, tap Place of Power to convert gold into VP, then buy a monument with remaining gold. Planning these chains before the round starts lets you execute them cleanly and race past opponents who are still calculating.

summary.title: Draft Archetypes
bullets: Death engine, Gold rush, Creature swarm, Dragon aggression
Identify which archetype your draft is shaping toward
Commit to 1 archetype — hybrid approaches are weaker

bullets: Cards passed to you signal what others are avoiding
Uncontested colors = cheaper Places of Power access
Adapt draft picks to table signals

Surprising VP bursts from efficient multi-step conversions

entries.definition: The most versatile essence type, represented by gold tokens. Used to buy monuments (four gold each) and pay many artifact costs. Widely accepted and easily converted.
Red essence tokens representing fiery energy. Used primarily for certain artifact costs and Place of Power claims. Generated by fire-themed artifacts and some Mages.
Green essence tokens representing life energy and nature. Used for certain artifact costs and Place of Power claims. Common in nature-themed and creature artifacts.
Black essence tokens representing dark energy. Used for death-themed artifacts and Places of Power. The Necromancer Mage specializes in death essence production.
Blue essence tokens representing water and healing energy. Used for certain artifact and Place of Power costs. Often paired with calm essences in nature-themed strategies.
A token that can substitute for any single essence type when paying costs. Extremely flexible and valuable.

The core engine-building cards. Each player has a personal deck of eight artifacts. Place artifacts by paying their essence cost to add them to your play area permanently. They generate income and provide abilities.
Your character card that defines your starting ability and essences. Each of the ten Mages has a unique power. You choose one of two dealt Mages during setup.
Powerful one-use-per-round effect cards. Eight are available in the display. Players swap Magic Items each round when they pass. First to pass gets first pick.
VP cards that cost four gold essences each. Provide fixed VP (typically one or two) but no ongoing abilities. Limited supply based on player count.
The most valuable assets in the game. Double-sided tiles with specific essence costs to claim. Provide VP, ongoing essence generation, and powerful tap abilities. Five are available each game.
Turn a card or tile sideways to use its ability. Each tap ability can only be used once per round. All tapped cards untap at the start of the next round.
When you pass, you stop taking actions for the round. Return your Magic Item to the display and select a new one. First to pass gets first pick.
The simultaneous phase at the start of each round where all players collect essences from their Mage, artifacts in play, and claimed Places of Power.
An emergency action where you discard an artifact from hand to gain one gold and one essence of your choice. Reduces your eight-card deck permanently.
Some artifacts and abilities allow you to attack opponents, forcing them to discard essences. The primary aggressive mechanic in the game.
Defensive icons on artifacts and Places of Power that absorb incoming attack damage. Each shield blocks one point of attack. Important for countering dragon strategies.
Your eight drafted artifact cards that form your entire draw pile for the game. You draw three as your opening hand and the rest stay as a draw pile. No other cards enter your deck.
The game ends when any player reaches ten or more VP at the end of a round. VP comes from Places of Power, monuments, artifacts, and certain effects.
Victory points come from Places of Power (highest value), monuments (four gold each), artifact VP values, and certain card effects that convert essences into VP.
A Mage that excels at converting one essence type into another. Provides flexibility to adapt to available resources and pivot between strategies.
A Mage specializing in death essences. Synergizes with death-cost artifacts and Places of Power that reward death essence investment.
A Mage that works with life and calm essences. Often paired with creature artifacts and nature-themed Places of Power.
Any ability that transforms one or more essences into different essences. Critical for adapting your production to match your needs. The Alchemist Mage is the best converter.
The core strategic arc of placing artifacts and claiming Places of Power to increase your essence income each round. A strong engine generates four or more essences per round by mid-game.
